query: management of laryngeal carcinoma with cartilage invasion

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Certain factors are considered during the workup of laryngeal cancer and in determining whether the patient is a surgical candidate. These factors help define the extent of oncologic surgery required and if any reconstructive surgery is expected to be needed, which include: Vocal cord mobility/function of the larynx Presence of cervical or distant metastatic lesions Involvement of the base of the tongue Involvement of the paraglottic and pre-epiglottic space Involvement of the thyroid cartilage Involvement of the carotid artery and sheath Invasion of the esophagus Invasion of soft tissue and adjacent laryngeal muscles Involvement of neck lymph nodes

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Cartilage -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As discussed, it is essential to understand the anatomy of the laryngeal cartilages as pathology can cause airway compromise. Pediatricians must be aware of this when dealing with epiglottitis and croup. Surgeons, anesthetists, or emergency physicians attempting to gain emergency airway access in cases of ‘cannot intubate, cannot ventilate’ require a working knowledge of the surface anatomy of the thyroid and cricoid cartilages. This knowledge allows identification of the cricothyroid membrane and a cricothyroidotomy to insert an infraglottic endotracheal tube. Otolaryngologists must have a detailed understanding of the laryngeal cartilages as anatomical landmarks during surgery and as sites of invasion of laryngeal cancer that significantly impact staging, prognosis, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Locally Advanced Laryngeal Cancers
retrieved_docs.content: Locally advanced cancers, inclusive of T3-4N1-3 disease, are more complex to treat and involve multimodality therapy (either surgery followed by radiation therapy or a combination of chemotherapy and radiation therapy). These cancers, if surgically resectable, are not typically amenable to laryngeal preservation surgery, while definitive radiation concurrent with cisplatin chemotherapy remains an option for laryngeal preservation. In contrast to early-stage disease, the therapeutic approach to locally advanced disease is based on level I evidence, with combined chemotherapy and radiation demonstrating improved locoregional control and larynx preservation in patients with a functional larynx and no erosion of laryngeal cartilages. In T4 disease, laryngectomy and adjuvant radiation therapy have demonstrated similar locoregional control rates compared to chemoradiation and salvage surgery but improved overall survival. Larynx-preserving chemoradiation is therefore not recommended for T4 disease and is associated with inferior survival unless the patient is not a surgical candidate for overall medical reasons. [43] [44] [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The management of laryngeal cancer in patients depends on the stage of the disease and the subsite of the tumor at the time of treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Glottic Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Early T1-T2a Glottic Cancers
retrieved_docs.content: Partial laryngectomy is less commonly performed for primary early glottic cancers but offers a viable alternative to radiotherapy and TLM in experienced centers. Systematic reviews have demonstrated comparable oncological outcomes in both de novo and radio-recurrent cancers to radiotherapy and TLM. [34] [35] However, appropriate patient selection is vital to achieving surgical clearance, as specific tumor characteristics such as subglottic or posterior commissure extension, cricoarytenoid complex fixation, or significant thyroid cartilage invasion are not appropriate candidates for partial laryngectomy. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with advanced-stage cancer can achieve comparable overall survival with either treatment regimen, except in specific scenarios. If the larynx is nonfunctional or at a high risk after nonsurgical treatment, laryngectomy is preferred to prevent chronic aspiration and pneumonia risks. [80] Additionally, for patients with T4 laryngeal cancer or T3 due to cartilage invasion, primary laryngectomy offers superior oncologic outcomes compared to nonsurgical treatments. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Locally Advanced Laryngeal Cancers
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperative radiation therapy is relatively indicated in the case of advanced tumor and nodal stage on surgical pathology (pT3-4, pN2-3), as well as other high-risk pathologic features, including close margins (<5 mm), perineural invasion, lymphovascular space invasion, extracapsular extension, and emergent tracheostomy due to morbid tumor invasion (to reduce the risk of tumor spread into the tracheostomy). Moreover, close or involved margins, multiple positive nodes, and extracapsular extension necessitate the addition of chemotherapy concurrent with radiation therapy, although induction, adjuvant, and neoadjuvant protocols have all been used with varying degrees of success when concurrent therapy is not possible. This scenario is often based on the physiological factors of a patient and may be somewhat predicted based on the patient's Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) status. [46] [47] [48] [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the signs and symptoms of laryngeal cancer during routine examinations. Implement appropriate diagnostic modalities, including direct laryngoscopy, biopsy, and imaging, for accurate staging and treatment planning. Select optimal treatment strategies, including larynx-preserving approaches for early-stage disease and multimodal therapy for advanced stages. Collaborate with multidisciplinary healthcare teams to develop individualized care plans and address the comprehensive needs of patients with laryngeal cancer. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Each primary subsite of laryngeal carcinoma carries different implications in symptomatic presentation, patterns of spread, prognosis, and treatment paradigms. Early-stage disease is often highly treatable or curable in the supraglottis and glottis, although the prognosis remains poor in the subglottis. Early-stage (stage I or II) laryngeal cancer can be successfully treated with monotherapy, meaning a single-modality of treatment, either surgical or radiation therapy, which typically preserves the larynx. [11] In contrast, advanced-stage disease (stage III or IV) carries a significantly poorer prognosis across all subsites, although the pattern is maintained. Glottic primaries often yield the best outcomes, followed by supraglottic and then subglottic tumors. [12] The treatment for advanced-stage laryngeal cancer warrants multimodal therapy, which may include surgery followed by radiation therapy, primary chemoradiation therapy, or a combination of all 3 methodologies (see Image . Laryngeal Cancer). [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Cricoid Cartilage -- Surgical Considerations -- Cricoidectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Cricoidectomy is a surgical procedure with partial or total excision of the cricoid cartilage. Cricoidectomy may be indicated in cases of subglottic stenosis, as the cricoid is the narrowest part of the airway [13] Cricoidectomy has also been considered an option in surgical resection of laryngeal chondrosarcomas, the vast majority of which originate from the cricoid cartilage. However, the role of the cricoid cartilage in providing structural support for the larynx as well as its role as a key attachment point for the arytenoid muscles is an important consideration for total cricoidectomy. In a series conducted by, De Vincentiis et al. , only one patient out of three was successfully decannulated after a total cricoidectomy; this is primarily due to the instability of the posterior wall of the reconstructed airway, which composed by the membranous wall of the trachea. This new membranous wall provides insufficient stability to the lumen and inadequate support to the arytenoid cartilages. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Arytenoid Cartilage -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Arytenoid cartilage mounted on the cricoid ring, with a functioning recurrent laryngeal nerve and lateral and posterior cricoarytenoid muscles form the cricoarytenoid unit a basic functional unit critical to phonatory and sphincteric functions of the larynx. According to this concept, surgeons should pay attention to the management of laryngeal cancer for the organ preservation; they should be able to maintain physiological functions of the larynx, namely speech, respiration, and swallowing, without compromising the loco-regional control of cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnoses often involve a history and physical examination, direct laryngoscopy with biopsy, and fine-needle aspiration cytology for nodal disease assessment. Treatment strategies are tailored to the disease stage and tumor subsite, highlighting the complexity and need for personalized management in laryngeal cancer care. This activity reviews the prevalence, incidence, associated risk factors, and underlying disease pathophysiology of laryngeal cancer, as well as thoroughly discusses the clinical presentation, diagnostic modalities, and available treatment options. This activity is designed to provide healthcare professionals with comprehensive knowledge and updates on the diagnosis, management, and treatment of laryngeal cancer. While highlighting the pivotal role of an interprofessional healthcare team, it also explores the latest advancements in the field and the long-term survivorship needs of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Papillomas -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Management
retrieved_docs.content: During the procedure, care must be taken to minimize damage to normal tissue since it may lead to an iatrogenic squamociliary junction, creating additional foci of possible papillomatous growth. Airway compromise is a serious concern when dealing with distant or large clusters of laryngeal papilloma. Nevertheless, during situations of airway compromise, endotracheal intubation with flexible bronchoscope or video laryngoscope should be attempted first to try and avoiding tracheostomy. Most laryngeal papillomas are easily displaced and manipulated since they do not invade deep tissues. Tracheostomy should be avoided unless completely necessary to secure the airway since it creates a transition zone and predisposes further spread of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Glottic Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Advanced T4 Glottic Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Chemoradiotherapy may be used effectively in some advanced T4 cancers; however, the high rate of the thyroid cartilage and neck soft tissue invasion means many patients will be unsuitable for laryngeal preservation and will require surgery in the form of total laryngectomy. The Veterans affairs study noted T4 laryngeal tumors had a reduced response to chemotherapy and a higher rate of salvage surgery, suggesting organ preservation is not effective in advanced laryngeal cancer. [37] Additionally, epidemiological studies have shown improved survival in patients with locally advanced cancers undergoing total laryngectomy compared to chemoradiotherapy, strongly suggesting surgery is the treatment of choice for T4a glottic disease. [40] [41] Total laryngectomy may also be considered in patients with significant laryngeal destruction, pre-laryngectomy tracheostomy, and non-functioning larynges. [29] T4b tumors are deemed inoperable due to the encasement of major vessels or the inability to achieve negative margins. Consideration of palliative treatment or chemoradiotherapy at this stage is feasible, and this has shown an association with a reduction of tumor progression and improved life expectancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Glottic Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cancers may eventually breach the glottis through a direct invasion of the vocal ligament and vocalis muscles, causing vocal cord fixation via involvement of the thyroarytenoid muscle. From here, tumors gain access to the paraglottic space, facilitating cranial and caudal spread. Laryngeal cartilage is relatively resistant to direct tumor invasion, although invasion of the thyroid cartilage will often occur in advanced T4a disease. While supraglottic cancers often exhibit early bilateral nodal metastasis and subglottic cancers invade paratracheal and mediastinal nodes, carcinomas of the glottis have a low rate of lymphatic spread, attributed to an inadequate submucosal lymphatic supply. Nodal metastasis tends to occur when the tumor breaches the anterior commissure or invades another laryngeal subsite, most often spreading to levels 2, 3, 4, and 6.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Papillomas -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of laryngeal papilloma. Outline the typical clinical presentation of patients with laryngeal papilloma. Review the management considerations for patients with laryngeal papilloma. Summarize the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to enhance care coordination for patients with laryngeal papilloma. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck represents a specific manifestation of سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck). This relationship indicates that the metastatic disease involving cervical lymph nodes is a subset of the broader category of head‑and‑neck squamous cell carcinoma, emphasizing the importance of evaluating nodal involvement for accurate staging, prognosis, and treatment planning in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is classified as a throat surgery and encompasses the specific procedure تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, which is listed as a child of Injection of larynx. In addition, Injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea is also categorized as a throat surgery. Both interventions involve therapeutic injections within the upper airway and represent distinct subtypes of throat surgery, illustrating the variety of surgical options available for managing laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx and injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea are both classified as throat surgery, representing surgical interventions that involve delivering agents directly into the laryngeal or tracheal structures of the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1315
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is classified as a type of throat surgery, indicating that this procedure involves an injection administered directly into the laryngeal structures as part of a surgical intervention on the throat.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 789
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are situated within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, indicating that examinations of the larynx and trachea are considered part of the general set of respiratory‑tract diagnostic activities. Therapeutic interventions such as Injection of larynx and Injection of locally‑acting therapeutic substance into trachea are each classified as throat surgery, reflecting that these injections are surgical procedures performed on the upper airway. Although throat surgery is primarily a therapeutic classification, it is linked to the larger diagnostic group of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, highlighting that certain interventions on the larynx and trachea can serve both diagnostic and therapeutic roles. Recognizing this overlap helps clinicians select appropriate procedures and coordinate care for patients requiring evaluation or treatment of laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of the larynx includes the subsequent step of تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway), indicating that airway management is an integral component of the laryngeal injection procedure. This relationship underscores that when clinicians perform a laryngeal injection, they also consider establishing an oral‑pharyngeal airway to maintain airway patency and support effective ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are all examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. Each represents a malignant tumor arising in a specific anatomic site—thyroid cancer that has spread to cervical lymph nodes, a malignant growth originating in the nasal bone, and a malignant tumor arising in the thymus. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their aggressive, disease‑causing nature and underscores the need for thorough oncologic assessment and appropriate therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 886
retrieved_docs.content: Squamous cell carcinoma of the trachea is categorized as a cancer of the respiratory and intrathoracic system. Patients with this malignancy may present with سُعالٌ (cough) as a clinical finding, making cough a potential indicator of tracheal squamous cell carcinoma and underscoring its relevance for early diagnosis and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: is likewise associated with the thoracic cavity, emphasizing that bronchial cancers arise within this compartment. Collectively, these entities illustrate a hierarchical framework in which anatomical structures (larynx, trachea, bronchus, thoracic cavity) and procedural categories (diagnostic, therapeutic, non‑therapeutic, throat surgery) are interrelated to guide respiratory system care and thoracic oncology.
